meta.title: Podiatry Practice Software | Diabetic Foot RTM & Wound Tracking | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to podiatry practice operations. $93K annual diabetic foot RTM. Wound healing measurement and tracking. DME coordination and diabetic shoe programs. Surgical authorization.

hero.subhead: Diabetic foot patients need systematic monitoring between visits — that's billable RTM that prevents amputations and generates $93K annually. Wounds need measurement tracking that identifies stalled healing before it becomes crisis. DME needs coordination from prescription to delivery. clinIQ manages all of it.

tableOfContents.title: The Podiatry Revenue and Clinical Challenge

sections.h2: The Podiatry Revenue and Clinical Challenge
sections.content:
Podiatry practices manage some of medicine's highest-stakes preventive care — the diabetic patient whose next wound could mean an amputation. They also run high-volume practices where operational efficiency determines revenue. These two realities create a dual challenge: capturing the monitoring revenue that diabetic foot care generates while managing the visit volume that keeps practices financially viable.

The average podiatry practice faces:

• 60-100 diabetic patients who need between-visit foot monitoring but currently get none — their calls come in when symptoms develop, not before
• Active wound patients whose healing must be tracked with precise measurements visit-to-visit to identify stalled healing requiring treatment changes
• DME programs (diabetic shoes, custom orthotics, offloading devices) that are poorly coordinated — orders lose in translation between the exam room and the front desk
• Surgical cases (bunionectomy, hammertoe correction, reconstruction) requiring prior authorization with conservative care documentation
• High daily visit volumes where operational efficiency determines how many patients are seen

60 diabetic foot patients enrolled in RTM at $130/month generates $7,800/month in revenue. That's $93,600 per year for monitoring work your team is already doing — phone calls, portal messages, walk-in concerns — just without systematic documentation or billing.

Diabetic peripheral neuropathy creates a dangerous situation: patients can develop serious foot wounds without feeling them. By the time pain alerts them to a problem, wound severity may already require aggressive intervention. Systematic monitoring between visits catches wounds at the earliest possible stage — and qualifies for RTM billing.

**What diabetic foot RTM monitoring captures:**

• Daily foot inspection: Any new skin breakdown, redness, blistering, swelling, drainage
• Neuropathy symptoms: Burning, tingling, numbness — changes in sensory status
• Temperature asymmetry: Patients guided to check if one foot is warmer than the other (early indicator of Charcot or infection)
• Wound monitoring (active wounds): Size perception, drainage amount and character, periwound changes
• Shoe and offloading compliance: Are diabetic shoes being worn? CAM boot compliance? Total contact cast intact?
• Glucose correlation: Blood glucose logging when combined with wound healing tracking

**Photo submission for wound monitoring:**

Patients with active wounds or previous wounds submit weekly foot photos through the clinIQ patient app. Provider reviews images between visits. Early changes — maceration, erythema spreading, wound color changes — are identified before the next scheduled visit rather than after.

**The clinical impact:**

Three categories of problems are caught through systematic RTM monitoring that would otherwise present as emergencies:

1. Pre-ulcerative lesions: Callus overlying a blistering area that will become an ulcer within days if not addressed
2. Infected wounds: Increasing drainage, warmth, color change — caught on submitted photo before sepsis
3. Charcot foot: Warmth, erythema, swelling in a neuropathic foot — temperature asymmetry monitoring catches this at Eichenholtz Stage 0

**The CPT codes:**

• 98975: RTM device supply and setup (one-time)
• 98977: Monthly supply with scheduled data recordings
• 98980: Treatment management, first 20 minutes
• 98981: Each additional 20 minutes for complex patients

**Related: [Diabetic Foot RTM: Billing, Enrollment, and Prevention](/blog/diabetic-foot-rtm)**

Wound documentation is both a clinical necessity and a billing requirement. For diabetic foot ulcers, precise measurement at every visit is needed to document healing trajectory, justify continuation of wound care services, and identify non-responding wounds that need treatment escalation or referral.

**What measurements matter in podiatric wound care:**

Linear dimensions: Length × width (longest × widest perpendicular measurement). Wound area calculated and compared visit-to-visit. Healing rate expressed as percent reduction.

Depth: Maximum depth in centimeters. Undermining and tunneling documented by clock position and extent.

Wound bed composition: Percent granulation (red), slough (yellow), necrotic/eschar (black), epithelialization (pink). Composition trending over treatment course.

Exudate: Amount (none/minimal/moderate/heavy) and character (serous, serosanguineous, purulent).

Periwound skin: Maceration, callus, erythema, induration extent.

Wound classification: Wagner Grade for diabetic foot ulcers, UT Classification, PEDIS — documented at each visit.

**Healing rate monitoring:**

The 4-week healing rule applies to podiatric wounds: wounds that haven't reduced by 40% in 4 weeks are at risk for non-healing and need treatment escalation. clinIQ calculates healing rate automatically and flags non-responding wounds for review.

**What treatment escalation looks like:**

Stalled wound at 4 weeks → consider: different dressing approach, debridement frequency change, offloading device change, infectious disease consultation for deep infection, vascular surgery referral for revascularization assessment, wound care center co-management.

**Photography tracking:**

Standardized photography at each wound visit linked to measurements. Photo-measurement pairing creates a visual record of healing progression that documents clinical rationale for continued treatment and supports authorization continuation.

Podiatry's DME program — diabetic shoes, custom orthotics, offloading devices — is a significant revenue source that most practices manage poorly. Orders get lost between the exam room and the front desk. Eligibility isn't verified before fitting. Delivery isn't confirmed. Patients don't follow up for adjustments.

**The Medicare Diabetic Shoe Program:**

Medicare covers one pair of therapeutic shoes and three pairs of inserts per calendar year for diabetic patients with documented foot comorbidities — prior foot ulcer, foot deformity, pre-ulcerative callus, nerve damage, poor circulation, or history of amputation.

The program requires:
• Physician certifying the medical necessity (PCP or managing physician statement)
• Podiatrist prescribing and fitting the shoes
• DME supplier delivering and billing Medicare

Eligibility verification, physician certification collection, prescription, fitting, ordering, and delivery — each step can fail silently.

**Custom orthotics:**

Custom orthotic pipeline: Casting or scanning → lab fabrication → delivery → fitting → adjustment. Each step requires tracking. Insurance authorization required for custom orthotics by most payers.

**Offloading devices:**

CAM boots, total contact casts, surgical shoes — each requires prescription, patient fitting and education, and compliance monitoring. Offloading compliance is tracked as part of diabetic RTM monitoring.

**What clinIQ coordinates:**

DME pipeline: Prescription ordered, eligibility verified, physician certification obtained, fitting appointment scheduled, order placed, delivery confirmed, follow-up visit scheduled for adjustment.

Annual program tracking: Medicare diabetic shoe eligibility resets January 1. Eligible patients who haven't received shoes for the year are identified and outreached.

Orthotic tracking: Custom orthotic status from casting through delivery with lab turnaround tracking. Authorization status tracked per patient.

Podiatric surgery — bunionectomy, hammertoe correction, plantar fascia release, Achilles tendon repair, reconstructive procedures — requires prior authorization with documentation of failed conservative treatment. The authorization denial rate for first-submission podiatric surgery requests averages 20-30%, primarily due to inadequate conservative care documentation.

**Common podiatric surgical authorization requirements:**

Bunionectomy: Most payers require 3-6 months of documented conservative treatment — wider shoes, shoe modifications, padding, orthotics — with documented failure to relieve symptoms. Imaging correlation (weight-bearing X-rays showing hallux valgus angle) must match surgical indication.

Hammertoe correction: Conservative treatment documentation — pads, wider shoe box, insoles — and imaging showing flexible vs. rigid deformity.

Plantar fascia release: Physical therapy documentation, orthotic trial, corticosteroid injection history, anti-inflammatory medication trial. Ultrasound or MRI documenting fasciitis.

Achilles tendon repair: Acute rupture vs. chronic tendinopathy — different authorization requirements. Acute rupture: minimal conservative care requirements; surgical choice documented. Chronic: conservative treatment documentation (PT, PRP, etc.).

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Conservative care log: Treatment documented at each visit — referenced automatically in LOMN for surgical authorization. Imaging results: X-rays, ultrasound, MRI linked to authorization documentation. Auth status: Submitted, pending, approved, denied, appealed — visible without calling payers. Pre-operative checklist: Medical clearance, lab work, consent tracked before OR scheduling.

Busy podiatry practices see 40-60+ patients per day. The visit mix includes quick nail care visits, complex diabetic foot examinations with wound care and debridement, new patient evaluations, and post-operative follow-ups. Managing this volume requires clear operational visibility.

**The podiatry visit flow problem:**

In high-volume podiatry practices, MAs don't always know who's next, providers don't know when rooms are ready, and patients wait longer than necessary because nobody has visibility into the queue. A 10-minute nail care visit that takes 25 minutes because of waiting bottlenecks reduces daily capacity significantly.

**Visit type differentiation:**

Nail care: Simple rooming, vitals optional, quick turnaround.

Diabetic foot exam: Full assessment, monofilament testing, ABI if needed, wound care if active wounds. 20-30 minutes.

Wound care debridement: Pre-set debridement tray, specific supplies, photo documentation. 15-20 minutes.

New patient: Full history, X-rays if needed, treatment planning. 30-45 minutes.

Post-operative: Wound check, suture removal, X-ray if needed. 10-15 minutes.

**What clinIQ manages:**

Queue visibility: Which patients are checked in, in which rooms, at which stage — visible to MA and provider simultaneously. Visit type tracking: Color-coded queue showing visit complexity. Room turnover: Room ready status visible. Diabetic foot exam protocol: Checklist ensures monofilament, photo, wound measurement are not skipped on diabetic visits.

**Week 1-2: Configuration**

Diabetic RTM program configured for your patient population. Wound measurement templates built for your visit workflow. DME coordination pipeline established. Surgical authorization tracking configured for your primary payers.

**Week 3: Training and Go-Live**

Clinical staff training on RTM enrollment and wound documentation. Front desk training on DME coordination workflow. Provider training on wound measurement tracking and authorization dashboard.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: Diabetic RTM enrollment begins. Wound measurement compliance from day one. DME pipeline active.

Month 3: 50+ diabetic patients enrolled in RTM. Wound tracking database building. DME order completion rate improving. First full RTM billing quarter showing $23,000+.

Month 6: RTM at $7,800+/month steady state. Surgical authorization approval rate documented improvement. DME annual eligibility outreach producing revenue.

**Annual ROI:**

• Diabetic foot RTM: $93,600/year (60 patients)
• Improved surgical authorization approval: $15,000-30,000/year in recovered denials
• DME program improvement: Practice-specific revenue capture improvement

**Investment:**

• Professional: $499/month — Full suite including RTM, wound tracking, DME coordination, surgical auth, patient flow
• Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: Is diabetic foot RTM covered by Medicare and commercial payers?
faqs.a: Yes. Diabetic foot monitoring qualifies for RTM under CPT 98977 (monthly supply) and 98980 (treatment management, first 20 minutes). The monitoring data — daily foot inspection results, neuropathy symptom changes, wound status, offloading compliance — constitutes non-physiological remote monitoring. Medicare reimbursement averages $120-150/patient/month depending on codes billed.

faqs.q: How does wound measurement tracking work?
faqs.a: At each wound visit, length, width, depth, tissue type percentages, exudate, and periwound skin are documented. clinIQ calculates wound area and healing rate compared to prior visit and to the 4-week baseline. Non-responder alerts fire when wounds haven't reduced by 40% in 4 weeks. Wound photographs are linked to measurements for each visit, creating a complete visual-measurement record.

faqs.q: How do you coordinate the Medicare diabetic shoe program?
faqs.a: Eligible patients are identified through diagnosis documentation. The program pipeline tracks: eligibility verified, physician certifier identified, certification obtained, prescription written, fitting scheduled, order placed, delivery confirmed, and follow-up adjustment scheduled. Annual eligibility resets January 1 — eligible patients who haven't received shoes for the year are flagged for outreach.

faqs.q: What surgical authorization documentation does clinIQ generate?
faqs.a: LOMN templates for bunionectomy, hammertoe correction, plantar fascia release, and Achilles tendon repair. Each template references conservative treatment history documented at prior visits — padding, orthotics, PT, injections — with dates automatically populated. Imaging findings, symptom duration, and functional impairment are auto-populated from the patient record.

The 4-week healing rule is built in — wounds that haven't reduced by 40% at 4 weeks trigger a non-responder alert on the provider dashboard. These patients are reviewed for treatment escalation: debridement frequency, dressing change, offloading assessment, referral to wound center or vascular surgery. Early identification prevents months of ineffective treatment.

faqs.q: Can patients submit foot photos between visits?
faqs.a: Yes. Active wound patients and high-risk diabetic patients submit weekly foot photos through the patient app. Photos are reviewed by the clinical team between scheduled visits. Early changes — maceration, spreading erythema, wound color change — are identified and addressed before they require emergency intervention.

faqs.q: How does high-volume patient flow work in clinIQ?
faqs.a: A shared queue shows all checked-in patients with their visit type, current stage, and room assignment. Visit types (nail care, diabetic exam, wound care, new patient, post-op) are color-coded. Room ready status is visible to both MA and provider. For diabetic foot exams, a checklist ensures monofilament testing, wound documentation, and photo are not skipped under schedule pressure.

Two weeks. Week 1: RTM program configuration, wound measurement template build, DME pipeline setup, surgical authorization tracking. Week 2: staff training by role (MA, front desk, provider) and parallel testing. Week 3: live with clinIQ support available.

cta.headline: See how podiatry practices capture diabetic RTM and prevent amputations through systematic monitoring

testimonial.quote: We caught three pre-ulcerative lesions from patient-submitted photos — caught before they became wounds. Three amputations potentially prevented. The RTM billing was the bonus.
